==Dietary biology==
{{Overly detailed|section|nosplit=1|details=|date=October 2024}}[[File:WedgetailEagleCarrion.jpg|thumb|A wedge-tailed eagle standing on [[roadkill]] carcass of kangaroo in the [[Pilbara]] region of [[Western Australia]]]]
[[File:Wedge-tailed Eagle, Dajarra, QLD imported from iNaturalist photo 200608451.jpg|thumb|[[Cannibalism|Cannibalizing]] a wedge-tailed eagle roadkill, killed when itself was feeding on a kangaroo roadkill]]
The wedge-tailed eagle is one of the world's most powerful avian predators.<ref name= Debus/><ref name= Olsen/> Due to its formidable and dominating nature, it is sometimes nicknamed "King of Birds", along with golden eagles.<ref>MacKenzie, D. (1964). ''King of birds: The story of the Wedge-tailed Eagle''. Geelong Naturalist, 1, 12–15.</ref>  Prey is usually grabbed via a pounce or snatch during a gliding flight or a tail-chase from low quartering or transect flights.<ref name= Ferguson-Lees/><ref name= Debus/><ref name= Aumann/><ref name= Cowell>Cowell, G. (2006). ''Wedge-tailed Eagle takes juvenile Kangaroo''. Boobook 24, 46.</ref> Prey is not infrequently spotted from a soaring flight and they may undertake a long, slanting stoop towards it.<ref name= Brown/> They may be able to spot prey from farther than a kilometre given their keen vision.<ref name= Olsen/> Its typical hunting style is not all-together dissimilar from that of golden or Verreaux's eagles.<ref name= Watson/> Occasionally, a wedge-tailed eagle still hunts from a perch.<ref name= Ferguson-Lees/> Unsuccessful hunts typically exceed in number successful ones.<ref name= Brown/>

Hunting habitat can be highly variably and can manage to capture prey in both open country and quite thick woodland or forest, though typically require an open understory in the latter.<ref name= Brown/> Almost all its prey is taken on the ground but to a lesser extent it may be taken from the tree [[Canopy (biology)|canopy]].<ref name= Ferguson-Lees/> They have been known to take birds such as [[currawong]]s and [[cockatoo]]s by coming around them by surprise around a tree or by darting out in flight at close range for a brief tail-chase.<ref name= Olsen/><ref name= Whelan>Whelan, D. (2009) Eagle takes cockatoo. Boobook 27, 16.</ref> Sometimes, an eagle may pull [[brushtail possum]]s and other mammals from [[Tree hollow|tree cavities]], as well as young birds from a [[nest]].<ref name= Ferguson-Lees/><ref>Mooney, N. (2013). ''Some observations of diurnal raptors catching apparently nocturnal animals in Tasmania''. Boobook 31, 61–62.</ref><ref name= Olsen3>Olsen, J., Fuentes, E., Rose, A. B., & Trost, S. (2006). ''Food and hunting of eight breeding raptors near Canberra, 1990–1994''. Australian Field Ornithology, 23(2), 77–95.</ref> They've been known to follow [[wildfire]]s to search for fleeing animals or alternately [[tractor]]s and other farm equipment for the same purpose.<ref name= Olsen/> Wedge-tailed eagles occasionally [[kleptoparasitism|pirate]] food from other predators.<ref name= Ferguson-Lees/><ref name= Olsen/> An eagle of the species can carry prey of at least {{convert|5|kg|lb|abbr=on}}.<ref name= Ferguson-Lees/><ref name= Debus/>

Large animals may be attacked by pairs or, occasionally, by groups acting [[Pack hunter|cooperatively]]. One record shows 15 wedge-tailed eagles hunting [[red kangaroo|kangaroos]], two actively chasing at a time, then repeatedly being replaced by two more from the circling group overhead.<ref name= Ferguson-Lees/><ref name= Olsen/><ref name= McGregor>McGregor, B. (2007). ''Wedge-tailed Eagle hunting behaviour''. Boobook 25, 14.</ref><ref name= Aumann2>Aumann, T. (2001). ''Habitat use, temporal activity patterns and foraging behaviour of raptors in the south-west of the Northern Territory, Australia''. Wildlife Research, 28(4), 365–378.</ref> Regardless of prey size and season, tandem hunts, mainly by breeding adult pairs or sometimes loosely associated young eagles, are not uncommon.<ref name= Debus/><ref name= Olsen2/> Of 89 observed hunts in [[Central Australia]] around one-third were cooperative ones.<ref name= Debus/> As in other tandem hunting raptors, one eagle typically lies in wait generally unseen while the other eagle distracts and drives the prey towards it.<ref name= Debus/><ref>Ellis, D. H., Bednarz, J. C., Smith, D. G., & Flemming, S. P. (1993). ''Social foraging classes in raptorial birds''. Bioscience, 43(1), 14–20.</ref> When hunting domesticated prey, they've been seen to land near [[livestock]] mothers to intimidate them and separate their young, so they can attack the latter.<ref name= Debus/> Sometimes, wedge-tailed eagles may use [[fence]]s to limit a prey's escape routes.<ref name= Debus/> In some cases, these eagles will attempt to force large prey such as kangaroos and dingos to fall off steep hillsides and injure themselves.<ref name= Olsen/>

At times, wedge-tailed eagles appear to hunt at earliest light or late twilight in order to come upon [[Nocturnality|nocturnal]] prey such as [[hare-wallaby]] and [[bettong]]s.<ref name= Olsen/> These eagles have been seen removing rabbits from traps and eating carrion in bright moonlight as well.<ref name= Olsen/> At times, remarkably, wedge-tailed eagles have been covering large prey with vegetation, apparently to [[Hoarding (animal behavior)|cache]] food too heavy to carry.<ref>Taylor, P. (2009). ''Unusual behaviour of Wedge-tailed Eagles''. Boobook, 27, 39.</ref> Carrion is a major diet item, also; wedge-tails can spot the activity of [[Australian raven|raven]]s around a carcass from a great distance, and glide down to appropriate it. Carrion consumption is recorded in all season and contexts, although generally non-breeding birds are more likely to [[Scavenger|scavenge]] and young wedge-tailed eagles, even more so shortly post-dispersal, are thought to be far more likely to scavenge on carrion than adults generally. Wedge-tailed eagles are often seen by the roadside in rural Australia, feeding on animals that have been [[Roadkill|killed in collisions]] with vehicles. The importance of carrion relative to live prey has not been greatly studied but away from human development, especially roads, carrion is less likely to be encountered and eagles of all ages must presumably hunt to survive. In general, Australian [[Accipitridae|accipitrids]] of many species not infrequently come to carrion and they along with large [[passerine]]s like ''[[Corvus]]'' species and [[currawong]]s probably fulfill the [[Ecological niche|niche]] that [[Old World vulture|vultures]] do in other continents to some extent, albeit with considerably less specialization.<ref name= Ferguson-Lees/><ref name= Debus/><ref name= Olsen/><ref name= Olsen2/>

Aggregations of wedge-tailed eagles may occur not infrequently at large carcasses, with up to 5–12 eagles or sometimes 20 gathering.<ref name= Ferguson-Lees/> A wedge-tailed eagle can gorge up to {{convert|1|to|1.5|kg|lb|abbr=on}} at a sitting and, when fulfilled, can lasts for an unusual amount of time, for up to weeks or even a month, before needing to hunt again, apparently due to the warmth of the environment.<ref name= Debus/> After feeding they may disgorge a relatively small [[Pellet (ornithology)|pellet]], {{convert|29|to|98|mm|in|abbr=on}} long by {{convert|20|to|50|mm|in|abbr=on}} wide and weighing some {{convert|8.8|g|oz|abbr=on}}.<ref name= Debus/> Usually the diet is determined from a combination of reviewing these pellets along with loose prey remains.<ref>Sharp, A., Gibson, L., Norton, M., Marks, A., Ryan, B., & Semeraro, L. (2002). An evaluation of the use of regurgitated pellets and skeletal material to quantify the diet of Wedge-tailed Eagles, Aquila audax. Emu - Austral Ornithology, 102(2), 181–185.</ref>

===Prey spectrum===
<gallery widths="200px" heights="155px">
Pogona vitticeps (32494027291).jpg|Regular wedge-tailed eagle prey can vary in size down to small [[lizard]]s such as [[Pogona|bearded dragons]], their favourite variety of reptilian prey.
CSIRO ScienceImage 1147 European rabbit.jpg|The [[Rabbits in Australia|introduction of rabbits]] to Australia has been greatly harmful to the Australian environs but a boon to opportunistic wedge-tailed eagles, which often take them in great numbers.
Large Eastern Grey male kangaroo (9645655070).jpg|Regular prey can range up to the size of large adult [[kangaroo]]s such as [[eastern grey kangaroo]]s, usually attacked in [[Pack hunter|hunting pairs]].
Galahs.jpg|A diversity of birds may be taken with medium-sized, common birds such as [[galah]]s taken relatively frequently due to the conspicuousness.
</gallery>

The wedge-tailed eagle is a dietary generalist, opportunistically capturing a wide range of prey species.<ref name= Debus/><ref name= Olsen2/> Its prey spectrum is quite broad, with well over 200 prey species documented to be taken and even this includes very few prey only from secondary accounts from Tasmania and New Guinea.<ref name= Debus/><ref name= Olsen/><ref name= Olsen2/><ref name= Olsen4>Olsen, J., Judge, D., Fuentes, E., Rose, A. B., & Debus, S. J. (2010). ''Diets of wedge-tailed eagles (Aquila audax) and little eagles (Hieraaetus morphnoides) breeding near Canberra, Australia''. Journal of Raptor Research, 44(1), 50–61.</ref> The wedge-tailed eagles tends to prefer smallish to fairly large [[mammal]]s as prey.<ref name= Ferguson-Lees/><ref name= Debus/> However, they not infrequently take ample numbers of both [[bird]]s and [[reptile]]s, along with scarcely other prey taxon.<ref name= Debus/><ref name= Olsen2/><ref name= Olsen4/> Out of 21 accrued dietary studies, 61.3% of prey items by number in the foods during nesting efforts were mammals, 21.6% were birds, 13.2% were reptiles, 2.1% by [[invertebrate]]s, principally [[insect]]s, 1.5% by [[fish]], and almost no [[amphibian]]s by number.<ref name= Debus/> Meanwhile, out of the 21, 13 studies calculated estimated [[biomass]], and found that just shy of 90% of prey biomass was made of by mammals, 6.2% by birds and 3.4% by reptiles.<ref name= Debus/> Out of the ''Aquila'' genus, it is one of a few generalist species, however the wedge-tailed eagle is the ''Aquila'' most likely to typically attack the largest prey.<ref name= Watson/> Generally, this species prefers to attack birds and reptiles weighing over {{convert|100|g|oz|abbr=on}} and mammals weighing over {{convert|500|g|lb|abbr=on}}, although prey taken at times has varied from a few grams to more than sixteen times the weight of an individual eagle.<ref name= Olsen/><ref name= Olsen2/>

A comparison estimate posited that around 2% of wedge-tailed eagle prey weighs less than {{convert|63|g|oz|abbr=on}}, 4% of their prey weighs {{convert|63|to|125|g|oz|abbr=on}}, 7% of their prey weighs {{convert|125|to|250|g|oz|abbr=on}}, 10% weighs {{convert|250|to|500|g|oz|abbr=on}}, 20% weighs {{convert|500|to|1000|g|lb|abbr=on}}, 25% weighs {{convert|1000|to|2000|g|lb|abbr=on}}, 18% weighs {{convert|2000|to|4000|g|lb|abbr=on}} and 14% weighs over {{convert|4000|g|lb|abbr=on}}.<ref name= Watson/> Projected from this comparison, the mean prey size for wedge-tailed eagles is estimated at {{convert|1750|g|lb|abbr=on}}, similar but just slightly ahead of the Verreaux's eagle and some 14% ahead of the golden eagle global mean prey size.<ref name= Watson/> Further studies estimated mean prey weight, showing the mean prey weigh in the [[Canberra]]-[[Australian Capital Territory]] region in three different studies was estimated to be {{convert|1298|g|lb|abbr=on}}, {{convert|2131|g|lb|abbr=on}} and {{convert|2890|g|lb|abbr=on}}, changing likely due to the shifting significances of [[Leporidae|leporids]] and larger macropods.<ref name= Olsen4/><ref name= Olsen5>Olsen, J., Fuentes, E., & Rose, A. B. (2006). ''Trophic relationships between neighbouring White-bellied Sea-Eagles (Haliaeetus leucogaster) and Wedge-tailed Eagles (Aquila audax) breeding on rivers and dams near Canberra''. Emu - Austral Ornithology, 106(3), 193–201.</ref><ref name= Debus3>Debus, S. J. S., Olsen, J., Trost, S., & Fuentes, E. (2021). ''Breeding diets of the little eagle Hieraaetus morphnoides and wedge-tailed eagle Aquila audax in the Australian capital territory in 2011–2019''. Australian Field Ornithology, 38, 19–28.</ref> In a small study from [[Armidale, New South Wales]], it was estimated that mean prey weight was {{convert|1309|g|lb|abbr=on}}.<ref name= Debus4>Debus, S. J., Hatfield, T. S., Ley, A. J., & Rose, A. B. (2007). ''Breeding biology and diet of the Wedge-tailed Eagle Aquila audax in the New England region of New South Wales''. Australian Field Ornithology, 24(3), 93–120.</ref> It only ranks behind the crowned eagle and harpy eagle and rivals the martial eagle as the eagle likely to attack the largest prey on average.<ref>{{Cite journal |vauthors=Shultz S, Noë R, McGraw WS, Dunbar R |year=2004 |title=A Community-Level Evaluation of the Impact of Prey Behavioural and Ecological Characteristics on Predator Diet Composition |journal=Proceedings of the Royal Society of London B: Biological Sciences |volume=271 |issue=1540 |pages=725–732 |doi=10.1098/rspb.2003.2626 |pmid=15209106 |pmc=1691645 |bibcode=2004PBioS.271..725S }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|author=Miranda, Everton B. P.|author2= Campbell-Thompson, Edwin|author3= Muela, Angel|author4= Vargas, Félix Hernán |year=2018 |title=Sex and breeding status affect prey composition of Harpy Eagles ''Harpia harpyja''|journal=Journal of Ornithology|volume= 159 |issue=1|pages= 141–150|doi=10.1007/s10336-017-1482-3|bibcode= 2018JOrni.159..141M|s2cid= 36830775}}</ref><ref>{{cite thesis|author=Hatfield, R. S. |year=2018|title=Diet and Space Use of the Martial Eagle (Polemaetus bellicosus) in the Maasai Mara Region of Kenya|degree=MSc|doi=10.13023/etd.2018.391 |publisher=Forest and Natural Resource Sciences in the College of Agriculture, Food and Environment at the University of Kentucky}}</ref>

====Mammals====
=====Introduced mammals=====
While the introduction of [[invasive species]] to Australia has been generally having a negative to devastating effect on native animals and ecosystems, the wedge-tailed eagle is one of a few native species to largely benefit from these introductions.<ref name= Debus/><ref>Woinarski, J., Burbidge, A., & Harrison, P. (2014). ''The Action Plan for Australian Mammals 2012''. Commonwealth Scientific and Industrial Research Organization Publishing (CSIRO Publishing).</ref> This is especially due to the introduction of the [[European rabbit]], which were deliberately introduced repeatedly (abortively in 1859 and then via a concerted effect from 1937 to 1950), largely so the wealthy could hunt them.<ref name= Fenner>Fenner, F. (2010). ''Deliberate introduction of the European rabbit, Oryctolagus cuniculus, into Australia''. Revue Scientifique et Technique, 29(1), 103.</ref> The wedge-tailed eagles quickly took to the rabbits as prey along with another introduced leporid, the [[European hare]] (''Lepus europaeus'').<ref name= Debus/><ref name= Olsen/><ref name= Olsen2/> In almost every part of Australia, these eagles take [[Rabbits in Australia|rabbits]] in some numbers and they usually constitute the bulk of the prey species in most, if not all, Australian food studies.<ref name= Olsen4/><ref name= Olsen5/> In some dietary studies rabbits have accounted for up to 89.2% of the diet by number and 86% by biomass, as in [[Bacchus Marsh]], however they more typically range from 16% to 49% of the diet by number in various studies.<ref name= Olsen5/><ref name= Foster>{{cite journal|author=Foster, A.|author2= Wallis, R. |year=2010|title=Breeding diet of the wedge-tailed eagle ''Aquila audax'' in southern Victoria|journal= Corella|volume= 34|pages= 45–48}}</ref><ref name= Hull>{{cite journal|author=Hull, C. |year=1986|title=The diet of the Wedge-tailed Eagle, ''Aquila audax'', breeding near Melbourne|journal= Corella|volume= 10|page= 21 |issue=4}}</ref> 

One Canberra study found that 98.5% of the rabbits taken were adults.<ref name= Olsen4/> In the largest study near Canberra, over 5.5 years, 19.3% of the diet of wedge-tailed eagles was rabbits (12.7% of prey biomass) among 1421 prey items, so the eagles took a total of some 275 rabbits in the 11 to 17 studied territories of the area.<ref name= Olsen4/> A study estimated that mean weight of wild rabbits in Australia was {{convert|1.4|kg|lb|abbr=on}}, lower than estimated in the past.<ref name= Sharp>Sharp, A., Gibson, L., Norton, M., Ryan, B., Marks, A., & Semeraro, L. (2002). ''The breeding season diet of wedge-tailed eagles (Aquila audax) in western New South Wales and the influence of rabbit calicivirus disease''. Wildlife Research, 29(2), 175–184.</ref> However, other studies estimated the mean weights of rabbits taken by wedge-tailed eagles as variously from {{convert|1.5|to|2.2|kg|lb|abbr=on}} or "usually over {{convert|1.65|kg|lb|abbr=on}}", infrequently reported to {{convert|2.47|kg|lb|abbr=on}}, size of the rabbits being perhaps limited the poorly-suited soil and environs of the Australian wilderness.<ref name= Olsen5/><ref name= Foster/><ref name= Sharp/><ref name= Parker>{{cite journal|author=Parker, B. D.|author2= Hume, I. D.|author3= Boles, W. E. |year=2007 |title=Diet of breeding Wedge-tailed Eagles ''Aquila audax'' in south-central Queensland|journal= Corella |volume= 31|pages= 50–62}}</ref> Meanwhile, the European hare is neither as widely established nor as prolifically taken as rabbits by wedge-tailed eagles but are by no means neglected and a substantial meal.<ref name= Olsen4/><ref name= Olsen5/> With a mean body mass of {{convert|4|kg|lb|abbr=on}}, hares have been as much as nearly 10% of the local diet and up to 14% of prey biomass in studies.<ref name= Olsen4/><ref name= Olsen5/><ref name= Foster/> [[Rabbit haemorrhagic disease]] was deliberately introduced to control the population of rabbits subsequent to 1995, followed more effectively by introduction of [[myxoma virus]] to limit the damage rabbits have inflicted on native vegetation and resultingly have competed native mammals like [[wallabies]] out of parts of their range.<ref name= Fenner/><ref>Mutze, G., Bird, P., Cooke, B., & Henzell, R. (2008). ''Geographic and seasonal variation in the impact of rabbit haemorrhagic disease on European rabbits, Oryctolagus cuniculus, and rabbit damage in Australia''. In Lagomorph Biology (pp. 279–293). Springer, Berlin, Heidelberg.</ref> Ultimately, the rabbit population may have more than halved and locally have been some 90% reduced.<ref name= Olsen/><ref name= Fenner/> As a matter of consensus, the wedge-tailed eagles do not appear to be adversely affected in major ways by the biological control of rabbits since they can revert to primarily taking native prey species quite readily.<ref name= Olsen6>Olsen, J., Cooke, B., Trost, S., & Judge, D. (2014). ''Is wedge-tailed eagle, Aquila audax, survival and breeding success closely linked to the abundance of European rabbits, Oryctolagus cuniculus?'' Wildlife Research, 41(2), 95–105.</ref> In the region of [[Broken Hill]], [[White Cliffs, New South Wales|White Cliffs]] and [[Cunnamulla]], rabbits have gone down from accounting for 56–69% of the diet to 16–31% of it.<ref name= Sharp/> Furthermore, wedge-tailed eagles have been known to successfully maintain population in the absence of any rabbits in a few areas.<ref name= Debus/><ref name= Fitzsimons>{{cite journal|author=Fitzsimons, J. A.|author2= Carlyon, K.|author3= Thomas, J. L.|author4= Rose, A. B. |year=2014|title=The breeding diet of Wedge-tailed Eagles ''Aquila audax'' in the absence of rabbits: Kangaroo Island, South Australia|journal= Corella |volume=38|issue= 1|pages=18–21}}</ref>

[[File:You gonna finish that ?.jpg|thumb|right|Wedge-tailed eagles are disproportionately likely to be seen eating [[carrion]] of [[roadkill]]s (killed by cars). In the wild they often capture live prey. They often tolerate sharing carrion with other scavengers, such as [[Corvus|ravens]], despite them being prey species as well.]]

Much more controversial at one time than hunting introduced rabbits and hares is the wedge-tailed eagle's occasional tendency to feed on and sometimes kill domesticated [[livestock]] animals.<ref name= Batey>Batey, I. (1907). ''Wedge-Tailed Eagle and Lambs''. Emu - Austral Ornithology, 7(1), 43–45.</ref><ref name= Winkel>{{cite journal|author=Winkel, P. |year=2007|title= Feeding ecology of the Wedge-tailed Eagle ''Aquila audax'' in north-west Queensland: Interactions with lambs|journal= Corella|volume= 31|pages= 41–49}}</ref> The predation of wedge-tailed eagles on young farm animals has been the primary historic driver for the persecution of the species.<ref name= Thirgood>Thirgood, S., Woodroffe, R., & Rabinowitz, A. (2005). ''The impact of human-wildlife conflict on human lives and livelihoods''. Conservation Biology Series-Cambridge-, 9, 13.</ref> However, in no known study have domestic livestock been known to be primary prey.<ref name= Debus/><ref name= Olsen/> The closest association with them was in northwestern [[Queensland]] where [[Sheep|lambs]] (''Ovis aries'') made up 32.7% of prey in pellets and 17.1% in remains, accounting for 15–21% of the prey biomass, while juvenile [[pig]]s (''Sus scrofa domesticus'') made up 7.3% of pellet remains and 22% of the biomass.<ref name= Winkel/> Although it can be highly difficult, attempts have been made at parsing out whether the eagles had indeed killed the lambs rather than just lifting or dismantling them after finding them dead, as this eagle quite readily comes to carrion. The findings were that of 29 diagnosable lamb deaths in northwest Queensland, only 34.5% were due to eagle attacks.<ref name= Olsen2/><ref name= Winkel/> 

The wedge-tailed eagle is at times capable of taking very substantial livestock animals, lambs taken have been estimated to weigh a mean of {{convert|3.5|kg|lb|abbr=on}} or up to {{convert|15|kg|lb|abbr=on}} while fully grown sheep weighing some {{convert|40|to|50|kg|lb|abbr=on}} are infrequently vulnerable, presumably in large part to hunting pairs of eagles.<ref name= Olsen5/><ref name= Debus3/><ref name= Sharp/><ref name= Winkel/> In the largest study of the Canberra area, 82.5% of diagnosable sheep specimens were adults but probably were by and large scavenged.<ref name= Olsen4/> Meanwhile, young pigs included in the diet were estimated to weigh around {{convert|14|kg|lb|abbr=on}}, and sometimes [[feral pig]]lets are included in the diet.<ref name= Parker/><ref name= Winkel/> When attacking lambs, the wedge-tailed eagles are apparently capable of driving their talons into the skull of the victim, although more typically they land along the back and grip the lamb along the spine until it weakens and collapses while flapping the wings for balance.<ref name= Batey/><ref name= Winkel/> This species will also land between a ewe or female pig and their respective lambs or piglets in order to separate the latter for attack.<ref name= Debus/> Wedge-tailed eagles are also known to at times prey on another animal introduced for human hunting purposes, the [[red fox]] (''Vulpes vulpes''), which can form up to about 4% of an eagle's breeding diet and 5% of the biomass, weighing up to {{convert|9|kg|lb|abbr=on}}. In Canberra, about 59% of the foxes found in the diet were adults.<ref name= Olsen4/><ref name= Debus3/><ref name= Brooker2>{{cite journal|title=The Diet of the Wedge-tailed Eagle, ''Aquila Audax'', in Western Australia|journal=Australian Wildlife Research|year=1980|first=M.G.|last=Brooker|author2=Ridpath, M.G. |volume=7|issue=3|pages=433–452|doi= 10.1071/WR9800433|bibcode=1980WildR...7..433B }}</ref><ref>Lewis, C. F. (1957). ''Wedge-tailed eagle takes a fox''. Victorian Naturalist, 74, 89–90.</ref> Additionally, [[feral cat]]s, mainly juveniles, can be part of their prey.<ref name= Brooker2/><ref>Sharp, Andy, et al. "The breeding season diet of wedge-tailed eagles (Aquila audax) in western New South Wales and the influence of rabbit calicivirus disease." Wildlife Research 29.2 (2002): 175–184.</ref>

=====Native mammals=====
Presumably, the primary native prey of wedge-tailed eagles is [[marsupial]]s, particularly [[macropodiformes|macropods]], which is also in accord with studies involving places where rabbits have declined or never occurred.<ref name= Debus/><ref name= Fitzsimons/><ref name= Richards>Richards, J. D., & Short, J. (1998). ''Wedge-tailed eagle Aquila audax predation on endangered mammals and rabbits at Shark Bay, Western Australia''. Emu, 98(1), 23–31.</ref> Many wallabies, kangaroos, and associated animals are included in the diet, with over 50 marsupials known to be in the species' prey spectrum. When selecting marsupials, wedge-tailed eagles tend to ignore smaller species and focus on larger-sized ones.<ref name= Debus/><ref name= Olsen2/><ref name= Olsen4/><ref name= Olsen5/> However, they generally most often take alive the young, small and sickly of large macropod marsupials.<ref name= Olsen5/><ref name= Cherriman/> Findings were that juvenile macropods were taken out of proportion to their numbers in the environment, unlike rabbits which were taken roughly in proportion to their abundance.<ref name= Collins/>

In recent times, they have been known to eat marsupials, such as kangaroos killed by cars. There is little evidence that macropods delivered to nests are usually roadkills or from carrion, but the source of prey is difficult to determine because, to minimize disturbance, examinations are usually done after breeding is complete. As well, the attendance at carrion by wedge-tailed eagles is disproportionately done by juvenile eagles. In one study of roadkills in Australia, the species ranked around fourth in frequency and capacity for carcass breakdown of scavengers at roadkills, behind feral pigs, red foxes, and ravens.<ref name= Aumann/><ref>Peisley, R. K., Saunders, M. E., Robinson, W. A., & Luck, G. W. (2017). ''The role of avian scavengers in the breakdown of carcasses in pastoral landscapes''. Emu - Austral Ornithology, 117(1), 68–77.</ref><ref>Brown, O. J. F., Field, J., & Letnic, M. (2006). ''Variation in the taphonomic effect of scavengers in semi‐arid Australia linked to rainfall and the El Niño Southern Oscillation''. International Journal of Osteoarchaeology, 16(2), 165–176.</ref>

A video surveillance study at the nest determined that seemingly freshly killed, albeit usually quite young macropods were delivered to nests near [[Broken Hill]].<ref name= Collins/> As much as 20% to 30% of the diet can be made up of by macropods.<ref name= Debus/> Large and prominent species are known including the [[macropus|grey kangaroos]] and the [[red kangaroo]] (''Osphranter rufus''). Generally, juveniles are targeted of these large species with [[eastern grey kangaroo]]s (''Macropus giganteus'') estimated to weigh {{convert|17.2|to|20|kg|lb|abbr=on}} when taken by wedge-tailed eagles, in Australian Capital Territory and New South Wales, while the weight of young [[western grey kangaroo]]s (''Macropus fuliginosus'') was said to be {{convert|3.8|kg|lb|abbr=on}} in one study in Western Australia.<ref name= Olsen5/><ref name= Debus3/><ref name= Cherriman/> The estimated weight of juvenile red kangaroos taken was {{convert|9|kg|lb|abbr=on}} in northwestern Queensland where they were the primary prey species ahead of lambs.<ref name= Winkel/> However, wedge-tailed eagles do not shy away from attacking large, adult macropods.<ref name= Olsen/><ref name= Olsen2/> Similarly large adult macropods killed by these eagles can include [[common wallaroo]]s (''Osphranter robustus'') (mean adult weight around {{convert|25|kg|lb|abbr=on}}), [[antilopine kangaroo]] (''Osphranter antilopinus'') (mean adult weight around {{convert|30|kg|lb|abbr=on}}), [[agile wallaby]] (''Notamacropus agilis'') (median adult weight around {{convert|16.8|kg|lb|abbr=on}}), [[black-striped wallaby]] (''Notamacropus dorsalis'') (median adult weight around {{convert|13|kg|lb|abbr=on}}), [[red-necked wallaby]] (''Notamacropus rufogriseus'') estimated to weigh around {{convert|16|kg|lb|abbr=on}} when taken, [[swamp wallaby]] (''Wallabia bicolor'') (mean adult weight around {{convert|15|kg|lb|abbr=on}}), and even red kangaroo adults.<ref name= Debus/><ref name= Olsen5/><ref name= Debus3/><ref name= Winkel/><ref>Blumstein, D. T., Daniel, J. C., & Sims, R. A. (2003). ''Group size but not distance to cover influences agile wallaby (Macropus agilis) time allocation''. Journal of Mammalogy, 84(1), 197–204.</ref>{{Excessive citations inline|date=October 2024}} 

Wedge-tailed eagles have been recorded attacking eastern grey kangaroos weighing over {{convert|35|kg|lb|abbr=on}}.<ref name= Olsen/> In extreme cases, they have killed kangaroos weighing approximately {{convert|60|kg|lb|abbr=on}}.<ref name= Olsen/> In one case, a huge male eastern grey kangaroo, estimated to stand {{convert|1.7|m|ftin|0|abbr=on}} was successfully dispatched by a pair of wedge-tailed eagles.<ref name= Fuentes>Fuentes, E., & Olsen, J. (2015). ''Observations of the killing of large macropods by Wedge-tailed Eagles Aquila audax''. Australian Field Ornithology, 32(3), 160–166.</ref> Furthermore, an adult female western grey kangaroo was witnessed to be killed "in a few minutes" by a hunting pair of wedge-tailed eagles, and the eagles are considered a serious predator of the western grey.<ref>Abbott, I. (2008). ''Historical perspectives of the ecology of some conspicuous vertebrate species in south-west Western Australia''. Conservation Science Western Australia, 6(3).</ref> In some unusual cases, wedge-tailed eagle hunting parties can form whilst hunting red kangaroos, sometimes including up to 15 eagles (more loose, opportunistic [[Aggregation (ethology)|aggregations]] than well-organized [[Pack hunter|groups]]), but usually only a pair is sufficient to kill such prey.<ref name= Olsen2/><ref name= Fuentes/> Normally, the eagles repeatedly attack the kangaroo, sinking their talons into the back or nape and then fly up, when the second eagle starts doing the same. In some cases, as many as 123 attacks have been carried out against large kangaroos before they succumb.<ref name= Olsen2/><ref name= Fuentes/> When attacking joeys, eagles may, in some cases, have intentionally caused a mother kangaroo to dislodge a joey from the pouch to capture and fly off with it.<ref name= Fuentes/>

In addition, several smaller and more elusive macropods are taken including [[tree-kangaroo]]s, [[Lagorchestes|hare-wallabies]], [[Bridled nail-tail wallaby|nail-tail wallabies]], [[rock-wallaby|rock-wallabies]], [[Macleay's dorcopsis|dorcopsises]] and [[pademelon]]s.<ref>{{cite book|editor=Martin, R. |year=2005|title=Tree-kangaroos of Australia and New Guinea|publisher= CSIRO Publishing}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|author=Hayward, M. W.|author2= L'Hotellier, F.|author3= O'Connor, T.|author4= Ward-Fear, G.|author5= Cathcart, J.|author6= Cathcart, T.|author7= Sephens, J.|author8= Stephens, J. |author9=Herman, K. |author10= Legge, S. |year=2012|title=Reintroduction of bridled nailtail wallabies beyond fences at Scotia Sanctuary-Phase 1|journal= Proceedings of the Linnean Society of New South Wales |volume= 134}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |author=Short, J.|author2= Turner, B. |year=1992|title=The distribution and abundance of the banded and rufous hare-wallabies, ''Lagostrophus fasciatus'' and ''Lagorchestes hirsutus''|journal= Biological Conservation |volume=60|issue=3|pages= 157–166 |doi= 10.1016/0006-3207(92)91249-R |bibcode= 1992BCons..60..157S }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|author=Pays, O.|author2= Dubot, A. L.|author3= Jarman, P. J.|author4= Loisel, P.|author5= Goldizen, A. W. |year=2009|title=Vigilance and its complex synchrony in the red-necked pademelon, ''Thylogale thetis''|journal= Behavioral Ecology|volume= 20|issue=1|pages= 22–29|doi= 10.1093/beheco/arn110}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|author=Wilson, G. R.|author2= Gerritsen, J.|author3= Milthorpe, P. L. |year=1976|title=The Yellow-footed Rock-Wallaby, ''Petrogale xanthopus'' (Macropodidae) |journal= Wildlife Research|volume= 3|issue=1|pages= 73–78|doi= 10.1071/WR9760073|bibcode= 1976WildR...3...73W}}</ref><ref>{{citation|author=Winkel, P. |year=1997|title=The ecology and management of the Proserpine rock-wallaby (''Petrogale persephone'') |location= Brisbane|publisher= Queensland Department of Environment and Heritage}}</ref><ref>{{cite thesis|author=Pentland, C. |year=2014 |title=Behavioural ecology of the black-flanked rock-wallaby (Petrogale lateralis lateralis): Refuge importance in a variable environment|publisher=Edith Cowan University|degree=Doctor of Philosophy}}</ref><ref>{{cite iucn |author=Aplin, K. |author2=Dickman, C. |author3=Salas, L. |year=2016 |title=''Dorcopsulus macleayi'' |volume=2016 |article-number=e.T6801A21952872 |doi=10.2305/IUCN.UK.2016-2.RLTS.T6801A21952872.en |language=en |access-date=27 March 2026}}</ref><ref>{{cite iucn |author=Leary, T. |author2=Seri, L. |author3=Flannery, T. |author4=Wright, D. |author5=Hamilton, S. |author6=Helgen, K. |author7=Singadan, R. |author8=Menzies, J. |author9=Allison, A. |author10=James, R. |author11=Aplin, K. |author12=Salas, L. |author13=Dickman, C. |year=2019 |title=''Thylogale calabyi'' |volume=2019 |article-number=e.T21873A21958741 |doi=10.2305/IUCN.UK.2019-1.RLTS.T21873A21958741.en |language=en |access-date=27 March 2026}}</ref><ref name= Cherriman2>Cherriman, S. C. (2013). ''Nest Sites, Breeding, Satellite Telemetry and Diet of the Wedge-tailed Eagle Aquila audax at Lorna Glen, Western Australia''.</ref>{{Excessive citations inline|date=October 2024}} Other marsupials are by no means neglected.<ref name= Olsen2/> In [[Shark Bay]], hare-wallabies and bettongs seem to form the central part of the diet.<ref name= Richards/> Another dietary favourite is the [[common brushtail possum]] (''Trichosurus vulpecula''), weighing some {{convert|2.55|kg|lb|abbr=on}}, which was important supplemental prey in the [[Perth]] area and was the primary prey species on [[Kangaroo Island]], at 33% of the diet there.<ref name= Fitzsimons/><ref name= Cherriman/> Around Perth, other small, nocturnal marsupials were taken in some numbers including [[woylie]]s (''Bettongia penicillata'') and [[southern brown bandicoot]]s (''Isoodon obesulus'').<ref name= Cherriman/> The [[common ringtail possum]] (''Pseudocheirus peregrinus'') was the second most prominent prey species in the diet near [[Melbourne]], comprising 20.1% of the diet, with some numbers of common brushtails also taken there.<ref name= Hull/> [[Long-nosed bandicoot]]s (''Perameles nasuta'') were regular supplemental prey in northeastern New South Wales.<ref name= Harder>{{cite journal|author=Harder, M. |year=2000|title=Diet and breeding biology of the Wedge-tailed Eagle ''Aquila audax'' at three nests in northeastern New South Wales|journal= Corella|volume= 24|issue=1/2|pages= 1–5}}</ref> Other notable marsupials known to fall prey to wedge-tailed eagles include adults of the following: [[koala]]s (''Phascolarctos cinereus''), [[quokka]]s (''Setonix brachyurus''), [[eastern quoll|eastern]] (''Dasyurus viverrinus''), [[western quoll|western]] (''Dasyurus geoffroii'') and [[tiger quoll]]s (''Dasyurus maculatus''), [[Tasmanian devil]]s (''Sarcophilus harrisii''), [[Macrotis|bilbies]], [[numbat]]s (''Myrmecobius fasciatus''), [[common wombat]]s (''Vombatus ursinus''), [[southern greater glider]]s (''Petauroides volans'') and [[Long-nosed potoroo|potoroos]].<ref name= Olsen/><ref>Melzer, A., Hodgon, J., Elliott, B. With relative infrequency, other classes of mammals, beyond leporids and marsupials, may be taken opportunistically by wedge-tailed eagles.<ref name= Debus/> At least two species each of [[Pteropus|flying foxes]] and [[wattled bat]]s are included in the prey spectrum.<ref name= Debus/><ref name= Olsen2/><ref>Welbergen, J. A. (2006). ''Timing of the evening emergence from day roosts of the grey-headed flying fox, Pteropuspoliocephalus: the effects of predation risk, foraging needs, and social context''. Behavioral Ecology and Sociobiology, 60(3), 311.</ref><ref>Cawten, L. (2014). ''Anti-predation strategies of chocolate-wattled bats (Chalinolobus morio) after a predation event at a maternal roost by a southern boobook (Tyto novaeseelandiae)''. The Tasmanian Naturalist, 136, 35–42.</ref> Occasionally, an eagle may take a [[monotreme]] including both the [[platypus]] (''Ornithorhynchus anatinus'') and the [[short-beaked echidna]] (''Tachyglossus aculeatus'').<ref name= Olsen4/><ref>Rakick, R., Rakick, B., Cook, L., & Munks, S. (2001). ''Observations of a platypus foraging in the sea and hunting by a wedge-tailed eagle''. Tasmanian Naturalist, 123, 3–4.</ref> Several species of [[rat]] are readily taken and even the [[house mouse]] (''Mus musculus''), likely the smallest mammalian prey known for wedge-tailed eagles at around {{convert|20|g|oz|abbr=on}} in weight.<ref name= Olsen4/><ref name= Foster/><ref name= Brooker2/> Although rare, a [[dingo]] (''Canis familiaris'') may be taken by a wedge-tailed eagle at times, mostly pups, or carrion but sometimes a pair of eagles can kill adults too.<ref name= Olsen/><ref>{{cite book|editor=Gompper, M. E. |year=2013|title=Free-ranging dogs and wildlife conservation|publisher= Oxford University Press}}</ref><ref>Burton, Maurice, and Robert Burton. The New International Wildlife Encyclopedia. Purnell Reference Books, 1980.</ref> Beyond sheep, pigs, and infrequently young [[goat]]s (''Capra hircus''), other [[Even-toed ungulate|ungulate prey]], entirely introduced by man into the Australasian region, is eaten exclusively as carrion so far as is known, including [[cattle]] (''Bos taurus'' - despite claims that eagles have killed young calves, which is possible, they have only ever been witnessed feeding on [[Placental expulsion|afterbirths]] and not harming calves), [[Javan rusa]] (''Rusa timorensis'') in New Guinea, [[sambar deer]] (''Rusa unicolor'') in northern [[Victoria (Australia)|Victoria]] and [[water buffalo]] (''Bubalus bubalis'') in the [[Northern Territory]].<ref name= Olsen4/><ref>Woodford, L. P., Forsyth, D. M., & Hampton, J. O. (2020). ''Scavenging birds at risk of ingesting fragments of lead bullets from kangaroo and deer carcasses in south-eastern Australia''. Australian Field Ornithology, 37, 112–116.</ref><ref>Hoogerwerf, A. (1964). ''On Birds New for New Guinea, Or with a Larger Range Than Previously Known''.</ref><ref name= Corbett>{{cite journal|author=Corbett, L.|author2= Hertog, T.|author3= Estbergs, J. |year=2014|title=Diet of 25 sympatric raptors at Kapalga, Northern Territory, Australia 1979–89, with data on prey availability|journal= Corella|volume= 38|pages= 81–94}}</ref><ref name= Rose>Debus, S. J. S., & Rose, A. B. (1999). ''Notes on the diet of the wedge-tailed eagle Aquila audax''. Australian Bird Watcher, 18(1), 38–41.</ref>{{Excessive citations inline|date=October 2024}} In one instance, a young girl was apparently subject to a brief attack by a wedge-tailed eagle, in what was likely an attempted act of predation, near her rural home but the attack was abandoned by the eagle. It has been noted that some different species of large eagles are thought to occasionally attack children as prey though, among extant species, only the [[crowned eagle]] and [[martial eagle]], both in Africa, are thought to have successfully carried out rare acts of predation on human children.<ref name= Olsen/><ref>Whitlock, F. L. (1928). ''Wedge-tailed Eagle Attacks Child''. Emu - Austral Ornithology, 27(4), 287–289.</ref>

====Birds====
[[File:Wedge tailed eagle (adults wing span can be up to 2 m) - panoramio.jpg|thumb|A wedge-tailed eagle feeding]]
[[File:Wedge-tailed Eagle It is Not Even Playing the Game! (50096251297).jpg|thumb|A raven [[Mobbing (animal behavior)|mobbing]] a wedge-tailed eagle, the latter being an opportunistic predator of many birds]]

Birds take a clearly secondary position to mammals when importance and especially prey weight are concerned; however, the wedge-tailed eagle shows some fondness for avian prey.<ref name= Debus/><ref name= Olsen/> With more than 100 prey species included in the prey spectrum, birds are the most diverse class of prey taken by these eagles.<ref name= Debus/><ref name= Olsen4/><ref name= Olsen5/><ref name= Brooker2/> Generally, the predation of birds seems to be highly opportunistic and no one type of bird reliably dominates the eagle's diet.<ref name= Debus/><ref name= Brooker2/> However certain species, probably due to their commonality in eagle territories and perhaps vulnerability through their own behaviour that seem to be taken most often.<ref name= Debus/><ref name= Olsen4/> These consist of ''[[Corvus]]'' species, especially [[Little raven|little]] (''Corvus mellori'') and [[Australian raven]]s (''Corvus coronoides''), weighing a mean between species of around {{convert|580|to|590|g|lb|abbr=on}} when taken, {{convert|300|to|329|g|oz|abbr=on}} [[Australian magpie]]s (''Gymnorhina tibicen''), {{convert|800|to|820|g|lb|abbr=on}} [[Australian wood duck]] (''Chenonetta jubata''), {{convert|310|to|335|g|oz|abbr=on}} [[galah]] (''Eolophus roseicapilla''), larger [[cockatoo]]s and smaller [[Psittaculidae|parakeets and parrot]]s.<ref name= Olsen4/><ref name= Olsen5/><ref name= Debus3/><ref name= Brooker2/><ref name= Dennis>Dennis, T. E. (2006). ''Status and distribution of the Wedge-tailed Eagle on the Fleurieu Peninsula, South Australia, in 2005''. South Australian Ornithologist, 35(1/2), 38.</ref><ref name= Fulton>{{cite journal|author=Fulton, G. R. |year=2019|title=Additions to prey taken by Wedge-tailed Eagles ''Aquila audax'' after release of Rabbit ''Oryctolagus cuniculus'' haemorrhagic disease (Rabbit Calicivirus) in 1996|journal= Australian Field Ornithology|volume= 36|pages= 11–12 |doi=10.20938/afo36011012 }}</ref>{{Excessive citations inline|date=October 2024}} 

On Kangaroo Island, Australian and little ravens together constituted 19% of the diet.<ref name= Fitzsimons/> In Canberra, fairly prominent numbers of magpies, wood ducks, galahs and [[Eastern rosella|eastern]] (''Platycercus eximius'') and [[crimson rosella]]s (''Platycercus elegans'') are known to be taken, these collectively forming up to about 25% of the diet by number.<ref name= Olsen5/> In the Perth region, birds were taken amply, especially the Australian raven at 12.6% of prey remains and 4.7% of the biomass, with birds constituting just shy of 25% of the diet.<ref name= Cherriman/> Elsewhere in [[Western Australia]], a similar percentage of the diet is made up of by birds, mostly the same species with some number of [[Australian ringneck]]s (''Barnardius zonarius'') and [[Baudin's black cockatoo]]s (''Zanda baudinii'') as well.<ref name= Brooker2/><ref>Saunders, D. A. (1982). ''The breeding behaviour and biology of the short‐billed form of the White‐tailed Black Cockatoo Calyptorhynchus funereus''. Ibis, 124(4), 422–455.</ref> Peculiarly, one study found that among a large sample of 1826 prey items in the [[Northern Territory]] that the most often identified prey species was the tiny [[budgerigar]] (''Melopsittacus undulatus''), at {{convert|29|g|oz|abbr=on}} one of the smallest avian prey species for this eagle.<ref name= CRC/><ref name= Aumann/> In a single study from the [[Fleurieu Peninsula]], birds were the majority of prey for wedge-tailed eagles, at 62.5%, mostly ''Corvus'' followed by wood duck, galah and magpies.<ref name= Dennis/> Other assorted avian prey include several species of [[waterfowl]], including several ducks as well as [[Black swan|swans]] and [[Magpie goose|geese]], and a fairly strong frequency of attacks on large [[Rail (bird)|rails]], such as [[Australasian swamphen|swamphen]]s, [[Dusky moorhen|moorhens]], [[Tribonyx|native-hen]]s and [[Eurasian coot|coot]]s.<ref name= Debus/><ref name= Cherriman/><ref name= Aumann/><ref name= Brooker2/><ref name= Corbett/><ref>Hardy, J. (1985). ''Wedge-tailed Eagle taking Black-tailed Native-hen''. Australasian Raptor Association News, 6, 14–15.</ref><ref>Smith, A. N. (2013). ''Wedge-tailed eagle preying on Purple Swamphen''. Australian Field Ornithology, 30(3), 157–159.</ref>{{Excessive citations inline|date=October 2024}}

Additionally, wedge-tailed eagles may take [[Australian brush turkey]]s (''Alectura lathami'') and [[malleefowl]] (''Leipoa ocellata''), [[stubble quail|quail]], [[pigeon]]s and [[dove]]s, [[Tawny frogmouth|frogmouth]]s and [[Australian owlet-nightjar|owlet-nightjar]]s, [[Pallid cuckoo|cuckoos]], [[buttonquail]]s, [[Banded stilt|stilts]], [[lapwing]]s, [[plains-wanderer]]s (''Pedionomus torquatus''), [[Bush thick-knee|thick-knees]], [[Silver gull|gulls]], [[Great-winged petrel|petrels]], [[cormorant]]s, [[heron]]s, [[ibis]]es and [[Royal spoonbill|spoonbills]], [[Crane (bird)|crane]]s, other [[birds of prey]], [[Laughing kookaburra|kingfishers]], [[honeyeater]]s, [[Chestnut-breasted quail-thrush|quail-thrushes]], [[Grey shrikethrush|whistlers]], [[Australian magpie-lark|monarch flycatchers]], [[White-winged chough|mudnesters]], [[Artamidae|artamids]], [[Eurasian blackbird|true thrushes]], [[Brown songlark|grass warblers]], [[Common starling|starlings]] and [[Richard's pipit|pipits]].<ref name= Olsen4/><ref name= Cherriman/><ref name= Sharp/><ref name= Fitzsimons/><ref name= Winkel/><ref name= Brooker2/><ref name= Harder/><ref name= Fulton/><ref>Priddel, D., & Wheeler, R. (1997). ''Efficacy of fox control in reducing the mortality of released captive-reared malleefowl, Leipoa ocellata''. Wildlife Research, 24(4), 469–482.</ref><ref>Pedler, R. D., Weston, M. A., & Bennett, A. T. (2015). ''Long incubation bouts and biparental incubation in the nomadic Banded Stilt''. Emu, 116(1), 75–80.</ref><ref>Brooker, M. G. (1969). ''The nesting of the Chestnut-breasted Quail-Thrush in south-western Queensland''. Emu, 69(1).</ref><ref>Cleland, J. B. (1908). ''Malurus fighting its shadow''. Emu, 8(3), 151–151.</ref><ref name="auto">{{cite web | url=https://www.peregrinefund.org/explore-raptors-species/eagles/wedge-tailed-eagle | title=Wedge-tailed Eagle | the Peregrine Fund }}</ref>{{Excessive citations inline|date=October 2024}} The smallest avian prey attributed to wedge-tailed eagles is the {{convert|10|g|oz|abbr=on}} [[zebra finch]] (''Taeniopygia guttata'').<ref name= Winkel/> Particularly large birds are sometimes taken of a few species. When it comes to the [[emu]] (''Dromaius novaehollandiae''), Australia's tallest and second heaviest bird, wedge-tailed eagles normally attack the small young but are capable of attacking adult emus more than 10 times their own weight.<ref name= Davies>Davies, S. J., & Bamford, M. (2002). ''Ratites and Tinamous: Tinamidae, Rheidae, Dromaiidae, Casuariidae, Apterygidae, Struthionidae''. Oxford University Press.</ref><ref>Eastman, M. (1969). ''The Life of the Emu''. Angus and Robertson. ISBN 978-0-207-95120-6.</ref> Two estimates estimated the typical body mass of emus attacked were merely {{convert|2.42|and|3.32|kg|lb|abbr=on}}, respectively, against an average of {{convert|34|kg|lb|abbr=on}} for adult emu.<ref name= Cherriman/><ref name= Winkel/><ref name= Davies/> As much as 4% of the diet of wedge-tailed eagles can consist of emu chicks.<ref name= Cherriman2/><ref>Christopher, A., Cameron, A., Balda, R. P., & Brown, J. L. (1975). ''Wedge-tailed eagle attacks Emu chick''. Sunbird: Journal of the Queensland Ornithological Society, The, 6(2), 30–31.</ref> Some of Australia's largest flying birds are also included in the wedge-tailed eagle's prey spectrum.<ref name= Debus/> These include the [[black swan]] (''Cygnus atratus''), estimated to weigh {{convert|5|kg|lb|abbr=on}} when taken, [[black-necked stork]] (''Ephippiorhynchus asiaticus''), which weighs at least {{convert|4.1|kg|lb|abbr=on}}, and [[brolga]] (''Antigone rubicunda''), arguably Australia's largest resident flying species of bird at a mean of {{convert|6.23|kg|lb|abbr=on}}.<ref name= Cherriman/><ref>Drummond, R. (2005). ''Wedge-tailed Eagle takes Brolga''. Boobook 23, 19.</ref><ref>{{cite journal|author=Clancy, G. P.|author2= Ford, H. A. |year=2011|title=Nest site selection and nesting behaviour of the Black-necked Stork ''Ephippiorhynchus asiaticus australis'' in northern New South Wales|journal= Corella|volume= 35|issue=4|pages= 95–100}}</ref> An unusually close feeding association with a very large bird is with the [[Australian bustard]] (''Ardeotis australis'') in northwestern [[Queensland]], where bustards were found to account for 13.4% of the pellet contents and 23% of prey biomass. That study calculated the mean weight of bustards taken as {{convert|8.97|kg|lb|abbr=on}}, indicating that the eagles were selectively predating the much larger male bustards.<ref name= Winkel/>

====Reptiles and other prey====
When selecting reptiles as prey, wedge-tailed eagles by far are most likely to pursue [[lizard]]s. The range of lizards they may prey upon is highly diverse in size and nature, with somewhere between 20 and 30 species known in the prey spectrum.<ref name= Debus/><ref name= Olsen2/><ref name= Cook>Cook, W. E. (1987). ''Amphibians and reptiles: predators and prey. Amphibians and birds. Bibliography of Herptological References in Australian Ornithological Journals''. Smithsonian Herptological Information Service, No. 71.</ref> The most preferred reptilian prey by far is [[Pogona|bearded dragons]].<ref name= Debus/><ref name= Olsen2/> Despite the small size of this prey relative to most mammalian prey, they can be key to survivorship in more arid vicinities such as central and western Australia where there is less diverse prey to pick from.<ref name= Olsen2/><ref name= Collins/> In video monitored prey deliveries at [[Fowlers Gap Arid Zone Research Station]], [[central bearded dragon]]s (''Pogona vitticeps'') dominated the prey composition, making up 68.2% of 110 prey deliveries and the only known instance of reptiles forming the bulk of wedge-tailed eagle diet.<ref name= Collins/> A different study from prey remains and pellets found the central bearded dragon to comprise 28.6% of the diet among 192 prey items.<ref name= Silva/> In south-central Queensland, the bearded dragon was the leading prey species by number, making up 26.9% of 729 prey items.<ref name= Parker/> In northeastern New South Wales, the [[eastern bearded dragon]] (''Pogona barbata'') was the second most numerous prey species behind the rabbit, at 16.6% of the diet.<ref name= Harder/> The bearded dragons when taken by wedge-tailed eagles have had an estimated body mass ranging from {{convert|80|to|320|g|oz|abbr=on}}.<ref name= Cherriman/><ref name= Winkel/> They also prey on [[jacky dragon]]s.<ref name="auto"/> 

Larger lizards are readily taken as well given the opportunity. [[Skink]]s are occasional supplemental prey, [[Tiliqua scincoides|common blue-tongued skink]] (''Tiliqua scincoides'') at around {{convert|500|g|lb|abbr=on}} can make up around 5% of the diet (in northeast New South Wales), while the {{convert|204|g|oz|abbr=on}} [[Centralian blue-tongued skink]] (''Tiliqua multifasciata'') was quite prominent in the diet in the Northern Territory.<ref name= Aumann/><ref name= Harder/><ref name= Meiri>Meiri, S. (2010). ''Length–weight allometries in lizards''. Journal of Zoology, 281(3), 218–226.</ref> In Western Australia, {{convert|617|g|lb|abbr=on}} [[Tiliqua rugosa|shingleback skink]] (''Tiliqua rugosa'') and somewhat smaller [[Western blue-tongued lizard|western blue-tongued skink]] (''Tiliqua occipitalis'') collectively comprised about 7.5% of the diet.<ref name= Brooker2/><ref name= Meiri/> Much bigger lizards are sometimes taken, namely [[monitor lizard]]s. Around 20% of the 231 prey items of in a Western Australian study was found to be monitor lizards, mostly {{convert|2.32|kg|lb|abbr=on}} [[yellow-spotted monitor]]s (''Varanus panoptes'') with some {{convert|821|g|lb|abbr=on}} [[sand goanna]]s (''Varanus gouldii'').<ref name= Cherriman2/><ref name= Meiri/> Adult [[Rosenberg's monitor]]s (''Varanus rosenbergi''), weighing around {{convert|1.1|kg|lb|abbr=on}} can be also taken.<ref>King, D., & Green, B. (1999). ''Goannas: the biology of varanid lizards''. UNSW Press.</ref> Even [[lace monitor]]s (''Varanus varius''), which weighs {{convert|6.3|kg|lb|abbr=on}} on average adults, can be a prey for this powerful eagle.<ref name= Harder/><ref name= Leopold/><ref>Geary, N. "Notes on the Wedge-tailed Eagle." Emu - Austral Ornithology 31.4 (1932): 288-289.</ref> Contrarily, lizards down to the size of a {{convert|20|g|oz|abbr=on}} [[pygmy spiny-tailed skink]] (''Egernia depressa'') and a {{convert|38|g|oz|abbr=on}} [[thorny devil]] (''Moloch horridus'') may be taken.<ref name= Brooker2/><ref name= Cherriman2/><ref name= Meiri/><ref>Pianka, G. A., Pianka, E. R., & Thompson, G. G. (1998). ''Natural history of thorny devils Moloch horridus (Lacertilia: Agamidae) in the Great Victoria Desert''. Journal of the Royal Society of Western Australia, 81, 183.</ref>

Beyond lizards, wedge-tailed eagles seldom seem to hunt other types of reptiles.<ref name= Cook/> They hunt a few species of [[snake]]s, mostly [[venomous snake|venomous species]], because they are prevalent in Australia. Snakes known to be included in the diet including [[tiger snake]]s (''Notechis scutatus''), [[eastern brown snake]] (''Pseudonaja textilis''), [[ringed brown snake]] (''Pseudonaja modesta''), [[bandy-bandy]] (''Vermicella annulata''), [[yellow-faced whipsnake]] (''Demansia psammophis''), [[red-bellied black snake]] (''Pseudechis porphyriacus'') and [[brown tree snake]] (''Boiga irregularis'').<ref name= Olsen4/><ref name= Brooker2/><ref name= Cook/><ref>Aubret, F., Michniewicz, R. J., & Shine, R. (2011). ''Correlated geographic variation in predation risk and antipredator behaviour within a wide‐ranging snake species (Notechis scutatus, Elapidae)''. Austral Ecology, 36(4), 446–452.</ref><ref>Caudell, J. N., Conover, M. R., & Whittier, J. (2002). ''Predation of brown tree snakes (Boiga irregularis) in Australia''. International biodeterioration & biodegradation, 49(2–3), 107–111.</ref>{{Excessive citations inline|date=October 2024}} [[Eastern long-necked turtle]] (''Chelodina longicollis'') have been claimed as prey in one report although any other confirmed cases of predation on turtles by this species are not known.<ref name= Debus/><ref name= Cook/> Notably, there are no reports of wedge-tailed eagles attacking [[Pythonidae|pythons]], despite several species being present in Australia, nor on [[crocodile]]s; perhaps these are the only predators too formidable to be attacked, as both of these reptiles can attain extremely large sizes.<ref>Harvey, M. B. (2001). ''Pythons of Australia: A Natural History''. UNSW Press.</ref><ref>Webb, G. & Manolis, S. C. (1993). ''Crocodiles of Australia''. Reed.</ref> Predation on [[frog]]s or other [[amphibian]]s is almost unheard of for wedge-tailed eagles, however, based on toxicity reports in eagles, they may consume invasive [[cane toad]]s (''Rhinella marina'') from time to time.<ref>Hill, A. G., Madden, C., & Reddrop, C.  (2021). ''Suspected cane toad (Rhinella marina) toxicity in a wedge‐tailed eagle (Aquila audax)''. Veterinary Record Case Reports, e159.</ref> Similarly rare in the species' diet is [[fish]], although [[common carp]] (''Cyprinus carpio'') and [[Achoerodus gouldii|western blue groper]] (''Achoerodus gouldii'') have been documented as prey.<ref name= Olsen4/><ref name= Brooker2/> Occasionally, wedge-tailed eagles may even attack insects such as ''[[Psaltoda moerens]]'' cicadas and ''[[Heteronychus arator]]'' beetles.<ref name= Olsen4/> Truly exceptional is in the Northern Territory, where a large percentage of 1826 prey items was made up of by insects including unidentified [[Orthoptera]], at about 10.8% of the diet, unidentified [[beetle]]s, at about 8.4%, as well as some numbers of [[ant]]s. Why and how they capture a profusion of insects locally is not clear, and they may be often from the stomachs of other prey or even byproduct from the captures of other prey or from the bodies of carcasses.<ref name= Aumann/>

===Interspecific predatory relationships===
[[File:Wedge-tailed Eagle - Australia 01 (15527144985).jpg|thumb|Being the largest and most powerful Australian raptor, wedge-tailed eagles are dominant over other raptor species of the region.]]

The wedge-tailed eagle occupies a fairly unique niche relative to other ''Aquila''. While primarily continental in distribution, it is distributed well apart from most related species, whereas most ''Aquila'' are distributed in [[Eurasia]] or [[Africa]] and face considerable competition over resources, enabling certain specializations of most species in habitat or microhabitat, morphology and behaviours and often life history, including nesting grounds and often foods.<ref name= Brown/><ref name= Watson/><ref name= Cramp>{{cite book |last1=Cramp |first1=S. |last2=Simmons |first2=K.E.L. |year=1980 |title=''Birds of the Western Palearctic'' |volume=2 |publisher=Oxford University Press |location=Oxford}}</ref> The wedge-tailed eagle has the ability to exploit a more catholic variation of both prey and habitat since it exists with relatively fewer competing species.<ref name= Debus/><ref name= Olsen2/> 

The most considerable potential competition comes in the two other [[eagle]]s regularly distributed in Australia, the [[little eagle]] and [[white-bellied sea eagle]].<ref name= Olsen4/><ref name= Olsen5/> The little eagle has a few ecological similarities to the wedge-tailed eagle. It is also something of a habitat generalist, although it is found somewhat scarcely in more arid vicinity, high elevation areas and varied semi-open forest than the wedge-tailed eagle.<ref name= Debus/><ref name= Johnstone/><ref name= Olsen4/> Like the wedge-tailed eagle, the little eagle has in recent decades become a somewhat specialized predator of European rabbits.<ref name= Debus/><ref name= Olsen4/> However, the size difference is extreme between the wedge-tailed and little eagles, with the earlier over four times heavier than the latter, and the little eagle as expected exploits a lower [[trophic level]] relative to its more powerful competitor.<ref name= Debus/><ref name= Olsen4/> As in other areas where booted eagles and [[sea eagle]]s have abutting ranges, sometimes wedge-tailed eagles compete with white-bellied sea eagles.<ref name= Olsen5/> 

One key difference from elsewhere where competition sometimes occurs such as the golden eagle with the [[white-tailed eagle]] (''Haliaeetus albicilla'') in Eurasia and the [[bald eagle]] (''Haliaeetus leucocephalus'') in North America, both of which are slightly heavier than the golden, is that the white-bellied sea eagle is the slightly smaller species than the wedge-tailed eagle, potentially giving the latter a more pronounced competitive edge.<ref name= Debus/><ref name= Olsen/><ref name= Palmer>Palmer, R. S. (Ed.). (1988). ''Handbook of North American Birds Volume VI: Diurnal Raptors (Part 1)''. Yale University Press.</ref> However, the white-bellied sea eagle clearly does not shy away from contentious border disputes with wedge-tailed eagles and the two species can often be seen be seen readily attacking each other, occasionally in talon grappling and sometimes cartwheeling attacks on one another.<ref name= Johnstone/><ref name= Olsen5/> However, the ecological effect of interspecific competition of the two species is not clear. Although the wedge-tailed eagle is considered the dominant species of the two, they clearly do not take the presence of white-bellied sea eagles lightly and some authors feel they may avoid nesting near them.<ref name= Olsen/><ref name= Olsen5/><ref name= White-bellied>{{cite journal|author=Olsen, J.|author2= Debus, S. J.|author3= Rose, A. B.|author4= Judge, D. |year=2013|title=Diets of White-bellied Sea-Eagles ''Haliaeetus leucogaster'' and Whistling Kites ''Haliastur sphenurus'' breeding near Canberra, 2003–2008|journal= Corella|volume= 37|pages= 13–18}}</ref> Clearly, there is ample partitioning between the wedge-tailed and white-bellied sea eagles, the latter adapted to mostly open [[wetland]]s and [[coast]]s and, while also a dietary generalist, they tend to derive most of their diet from [[fish]], [[water bird]]s and other wetland-dwelling prey, and they seldom compete directly for prey such as mammals with wedge-tailed eagles.<ref name= Olsen5/><ref name= White-bellied/>

Most other diurnal raptors that reside in Australia are considerably smaller and seldom can be said to present great competition to the wedge-tailed eagles, although some, such as [[swamp harrier]]s (''Circus approximans''), [[black-breasted kite]]s and [[grey goshawk]]s (''Accipiter novaehollandiae''), are relatively large for their taxon and powerful predators in their own rights.<ref name= Debus/><ref name= Olsen2/> In one instance, a [[square-tailed kite]] (''Lophoictinia isura'') was observed to engage in an apparent territorial fight with a wedge-tailed eagle, including talon-grappling.<ref>Raymond, A., & Morrison, V. (1990). ''Encounter between square-tailed kite Lophoictinia isura and wedge-tailed eagle Aquila audax''. Australian Bird Watcher, 13(7), 238.</ref> When it comes to carrion, wedge-tailed eagles tend to dominate other predators, especially most birds, with most kites, other assorted raptors and some large [[passerine]] birds, mainly ''Corvus'' species and [[butcherbird]]s, coming to dead animals including roadkills.<ref name= Olsen2/><ref>Selva, N., Moleón, M., Sebastián-González, E., DeVault, T. L., Quaggiotto, M. M., Bailey, D. M., Lambertucci , S. A. & Margalida, A. (2019). ''Vertebrate scavenging communities''. In Carrion Ecology and Management (pp. 71–99). Springer, Cham.</ref> However, heavier terrestrial meat-eaters can hold their own at times against wedge-tailed eagles, namely red foxes, dingos, monitor lizards and Tasmanian devils, despite all these species sometimes turning up as prey of these eagles as well.<ref name= Debus/><ref name= Olsen/> 

Sometimes the wedge-tailed eagle will readily rob various other raptors of their prey, including little eagles, white-bellied sea eagles and [[brown falcon]]s (''Falco berigora'').<ref name= Debus/><ref name= Olsen3/><ref>Debus, S. (2006). ''Wedge-tailed Eagle robs Little Eagle''. Boobook, 24, 43.</ref><ref>Stephenson, D. (2010). ''Sea-Eagles''. Bird Observer 867, 27.</ref> Wedge-tailed eagles will opportunistically prey on other birds of prey. They share this aptitude with other large eagles in different parts of the world such as golden eagles, although such acts are relatively infrequent, it is clear that the wedge-tailed eagle is considered a primary threat by many raptors based on witnessed attacks by eagles on them and the mobbing behaviour of other raptors.<ref name= Debus/><ref name= Watson/><ref name= Ley>Ley, A. (2006). ''Wedge-tailed Eagles preying on other raptors''. Boobook 24, 42.</ref> Among the other birds of prey known to occasionally fall prey to these eagles are little eagles, [[collared sparrowhawk]]s (''Accipiter cirrocephalus''), [[grey goshawk]]s, [[brown goshawk]]s (''Accipiter fasciatus''), [[Pacific baza]]s (''Aviceda subcristata''), [[black-breasted buzzard]]s, [[peregrine falcon]]s (''Falco peregrinus''), [[Australian hobby|Australian hobbies]] (''Falco longipennis''), [[black falcon]]s (''Falco subniger''), [[brown falcon]]s and [[Nankeen kestrel]]s (''Falco cenchroides'').<ref name= Debus/><ref name= Olsen3/><ref name= Olsen4/><ref name= Brooker2/><ref name= Harder/><ref>{{cite journal|author=Mourik, V.|author2= Richards, A. O. |year=2019|title=Predation of a Grey Goshawk ''Accipiter novaehollandiae'' with a Powerful Owl ''Ninox strenua'' as the likely predator|journal= Australian Field Ornithology|volume= 36|pages= 5–10 |doi= 10.20938/afo36005010 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|author=Watson, C.|author2= Nunn, P. J.|author3= Carter, M.|author4= Waring, R. |year=2016|title=Further observations on Black-breasted Buzzards ''Hamirostra melanosternon'' breeding near Alice Springs, Northern Territory|journal= Australian Field Ornithology|volume= 33|pages= 217–221|doi= 10.20938/afo33217221}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|author=Debus, S. J.|author2= Bauer, A. L.|author3= Mitchell, G. I. |year=2017|title=Breeding biology, behaviour and foraging ecology of the Black Falcon ''Falco subniger'' near Tamworth, New South Wales|journal= Corella}}</ref>{{Excessive citations inline|date=October 2024}} 

Occasionally owls are also included in the prey spectrum when an opportunity arises, including [[Eastern barn owl|barn owl]]s (''Tyto javanica''), [[southern boobook]]s (''Ninox boobook'') and even [[powerful owl]]s (''Ninox strenua'').<ref name= Debus/><ref name= Cherriman/><ref name= Cherriman2/><ref>{{cite journal |author=McNabb, E. G.|author2= Kavanagh, R. P.|author3= Craig, S. |year=2007|title=Further observations on the breeding biology of the Powerful Owl (''Ninox strenua'') in south-eastern Australia|journal= Corella|volume= 3|issue=1|pages= 6–9}}</ref> Wedge-tailed eagles are [[apex predator]]s and have no well-documented predators, although presumably they have some nest predators, likely including ravens and currawongs, especially when displaced by human disturbance from their nests.<ref name= Olsen/><ref name= Olsen2/> Occasionally, these eagles may possibly risk injury or death in conflicts against other powerful predators and scavengers, such as dingos, quolls, Tasmanian devils, goannas and snakes, but no such verified instances seem to be known in literature, and man is considered to be the wedge-tailed eagle's only true threat.<ref name= Debus/><ref name= Olsen/><ref name= Olsen2/> Occasionally they may be injured and even killed via intra- and interspecies territorial conflicts and [[Mobbing (animal behavior)|mobbing]] by other birds of prey, especially stooping [[peregrine falcon]]s which have successfully knocked wedge-tailed eagles out of the sky, with a force known to kill both golden and bald eagles in other parts of the world. Due to the formidable aerial attack of the peregrine, it may be the only raptor besides the white-bellied sea eagle that wedge-tailed eagles may avoid nesting near.<ref name= Olsen/><ref name= Olsen2/><ref>White, C. M., N. J. Clum, T. J. Cade, and W. G. Hunt (2020). ''Peregrine Falcon (Falco peregrinus)'', version 1.0. In Birds of the World (S. M. Billerman, Editor). Cornell Lab of Ornithology, Ithaca, NY, USA. https://doi.org/10.2173/bow.perfal.01</ref> Most of the large falcons, including peregrine, brown and black falcons, and at times large owls nest in unused or abandoned wedge-tailed eagle nests.<ref name= Olsen/><ref name= Olsen2/>

==Breeding==
{{Overly detailed|section|nosplit=1|details=|date=October 2024}}[[File:Aquila audax nest 01 Pengo.jpg|thumb|left|A wedge-tailed eagle nest in [[Barmah National Park]]]]

The breeding season is from July to December through much of range, in New Guinea apparently from May on.<ref name= Ferguson-Lees/> They have a distinct tendency to lay earlier in the more northerly part of the range. For instance, in northeastern Australia laying has been recorded in January and February and in Tasmania in September.<ref name= Ferguson-Lees/><ref name= Brown/> In western Australia, breeding depends on food and during drought periods there may be no nesting for up to 4 years.<ref name= Ferguson-Lees/> Adult wedge-tailed eagles are usually solitary or occur in pairs but immatures are more gregarious. 10–15 young wedge-tailed eagles may rest or soar together or even hunt together and up to 40 have been recorded at once at a carcass.<ref name= Ferguson-Lees/> Mated adults perform mutual soaring, undulating dives, and tandem flights with rolling and foot-touching.<ref name= Debus3/><ref name= Cherriman3/> The female may appear to ignore or more often turn over and present claws when a male is displaying.<ref name= Brown/> As possibly part of courtship, feedings on pairs have taken place away from the nest and sharing of a cache of food may occur.<ref name= Olsen/><ref name= Trost>Olsen, J. & Trost, S. (2014). ''Courtship feeding in Wedge-tailed Eagles''. Boobook, 32: 12–13.</ref> 

[[Preening|Allopreening]] occurs occasionally between pairs but is seldom observed, although at times has been considered a "regular" part of the courtship process.<ref name= Olsen/><ref name= Debus3/> Contrary to historical accounts, wedge-tailed eagles seldom engage in an elaborate courtship display and will instead generally try to conserve energy, instead devoting their energy for the upcoming trying breeding season along with territorial exclusions of conspecifics and obtaining food.<ref>Brooker, M. G. (1974). ''Field observations of the behaviour of the Wedge-tailed Eagle''. Emu, 74(1), 39–42.</ref> Mating tends to occur on a bare branch or dead tree in the nest area, and may continue into the nestling period.<ref name= Debus3/><ref name= Allott>Allott, M., Allott, M., & Hatchett, N. (2006). ''The breeding cycle of a pair of wedge-tailed eagles Aquila audax in South-east Queensland''. Sunbird: Journal of the Queensland Ornithological Society. 36(1), 37–41.</ref> Contrary to old accounts, the species does not mate in flight.<ref name= Olsen/> In the pre-laying phase, mating was recorded to be preceded or accompanied by loud, slow yelping, but in the nestling period, the pair alighted together and the male mounted without preliminaries and a silent copulation lasted for one minute.<ref name= Debus/><ref name= Debus3/>

===Territories and home ranges===
Territories are established with aerial displays, which can include high circling by one or both of pair, sometimes interspersed with flight rolls and talon presenting.<ref name= Ferguson-Lees/> Most of the time, wedge-tailed eagles typically respect pair boundaries and can limit territorial behaviour to mild aerial flights, with the intruders usually giving the ground to incumbents.<ref name= Olsen/> Violence is usually avoided but sometimes the most heated territorial disputes can escalate to deaths.<ref name= Olsen/> Sometimes the displaying eagle may engage in a steep dive on part closed wings followed by an upwards swoop, later may escalate into spectacular sky dance with undulations; they may too [[Aerobatic maneuver|loop-the-loop]].<ref name= Ferguson-Lees/><ref name= Brown/> Cartwheeling is typically rare but in one case, three immatures mock dived at each other, two birds interlocked and cartwheeled several times before breaking away. No cartwheeling or talon grappling has been reported between members of a mated pair, but occasionally reported as used against intruding eagles.<ref name= Ferguson-Lees/><ref name= Cherriman3>Cherriman, S. C., Foster, A., & Debus, S. J. (2009). ''Supplementary Notes on the Breeding Behaviour of Wedge-tailed Eagles Aquila audax''. Australian Field Ornithology, 26(4), 142–147.</ref> Aerial displays may go on for a while normally early in the breeding season, between 3 months and 3 weeks prior to egg laying.<ref name= Brown/>

Territorial attacks by male wedge-tailed eagles may be against any encountered intruding eagles, including both male and female intruders, while female eagles engage in less territorial attacks and when they do, it is exclusively against other females.<ref>Hughes, P., & Hughes, B. (1984). ''Notes on the territorial defence and nest building behaviour of wedge-tailed eagles''. Australian Bird Watcher, 10(5), 166–167.</ref> Territorial aggression can extend towards hang gliders and aircraft, advances noisily, bill open and talons extended until flying just above and behind or slightly ahead of pilot then swoops repeatedly after making contact with the hang-glider.<ref name= Ferguson-Lees/> A core of some {{convert|1|km|mi|abbr=on}} radius around the nest is most fervently defended.<ref name= Debus3/> Foraging ranges from nest may be up to about {{convert|20|km2|sqmi|abbr=on}}.<ref name= Dennis/> Foraging ranges on breeding home ranges may be around {{convert|50|km2|sqmi|abbr=on}} for males and {{convert|20|km2|sqmi|abbr=on}} for females in arid central western Australia.<ref name= Cherriman2/> Range sizes of pair members vary greatly based on topography, habitat and prey access.<ref name= Olsen/> Several reported densities of 3–6 pairs per {{convert|100|km2|sqmi|abbr=on}}, others of 7–12 pairs per {{convert|100|km2|sqmi|abbr=on}}.<ref name= Ferguson-Lees/> When rabbits were in plague type numbers, pairs may nest as close {{convert|700|m|ft|abbr=on}} apart and 4 others no more than {{convert|2|km|mi|abbr=on}} from those two pairs.<ref name= Ferguson-Lees/> In semi-arid areas of New South Wales near [[Menindee, New South Wales|Menindee]] densities were found to be about a pair per {{convert|53|km2|sqmi|abbr=on}}, 10–12 pairs in good years, 3 in drought years.<ref name= Robertson>Robertson, G. (1987). ''Effects of drought on a breeding population of Wedge-tailed Eagles Aquila audax''. Emu 87, 220–223. doi:10.1071/MU9870220</ref> Not far from that in [[Mutawintji National Park]] density was around a pair per {{convert|3|to|9|km2|sqmi|abbr=on}}.<ref name=Sharp3/> Much higher densities were noted in this semi-arid zone of western New South Wales, with a pair per {{convert|3|to|9|km2|sqmi|abbr=on}}, against around a pair per {{convert|40|to|48|km2|sqmi|abbr=on}} in other arid zones.<ref name="Sharp3"/> 

In Western Australia, arid areas had a nearest neighbour distance of {{convert|5.32|km|mi|abbr=on}} while those nesting in [[Mesic habitat|mesic areas]] had a distance of {{convert|4.88|km|mi|abbr=on}}.<ref name= Fleming>Cherriman, S. C., Fleming, P. A., Shephard, J. M., & Olsen, P. D. (2021). ''Climate influences productivity but not breeding density of wedge‐tailed eagles Aquila audax in arid and mesic Western Australia''. Austral Ecology.</ref> At [[Fowlers Gap Arid Zone Research Station|Fowlers Gap]], there were 9–10 pairs per {{convert|390|km2|sqmi|abbr=on}}.<ref name= Collins/><ref name= Silva/> Near Canberra, around 37 pairs were reported in an area of {{convert|1000|km2|sqmi|abbr=on}}, including some unusually as close as {{convert|130|m|ft|abbr=on}} to [[Road surface|paved roads]] and as close as {{convert|260|m|ft|abbr=on}} from suburban spots. This contrasts strongly with 36 years prior, when few nests were near human-altered areas and the amount of pairs in the same area was about 32.<ref name= Fuentes2>{{cite journal|author=Fuentes, E.|author2= Olsen, J.|author3= Rose, A. B. |year=2007|title=Diet, occupancy and breeding success of Wedge-tailed Eagles ''Aquila audax'' near Canberra, Australia, 2002–2003: four decades after Leopold and Wolfe |journal=Corella|volume= 31|pages= 65–73}}</ref><ref name= Leopold>Leopold, A. S., & Wolfe, T. O. (1970). ''Food habits of nesting Wedge-tailed Eagles, Aquila audax, in south-eastern Australia''. CSIRO Wildlife Research, 15(1): 1–17.</ref> In the [[Fleurieu Peninsula]] in [[South Australia]] during the early to mid 2000s, there was a pair per {{convert|53|km2|sqmi|abbr=on}}, active nest sites were {{convert|6.6|km|mi|abbr=on}} apart, while the average home range around the nest is {{convert|34|km2|sqmi|abbr=on}} roughly.<ref name= Dennis/> Resurvey efforts a dozen years later in Fleurieu Peninsula found a more populous population, resulting in a home range estimated at {{convert|32.1|km2|sqmi|abbr=on}} with some active nests as close as {{convert|1.5|km|mi|abbr=on}} apart.<ref name= Rowe>Rowe E., Brinsley, R., & Dennis, T. (2018). ''A review of Wedge-tailed Eagle population stability in the Fleurieu Peninsula region of South Australia in 2017''. South Australian Ornithologist, 43: 1–2.</ref> In the [[Perth]] area, it was projected that the mean home range was about {{convert|36|km2|sqmi|abbr=on}}.<ref name= Cherriman/> Meanwhile, in southern Victoria the nearest neighbour distance of breeding pairs was {{convert|4.7|km|mi|abbr=on}} while mean territory size was calculated at {{convert|17.6|km2|sqmi|abbr=on}}.<ref name= Foster2>{{cite journal|author=Foster, A.|author2= Wallis, R. |year=2010|title=Nest-site characteristics of the Wedge-tailed Eagle ''Aquila audax'' in southern Victoria|journal= Corella|volume= 34 |pages= 36–44}}</ref>

===Nests===
[[File:Mutawintji4 - Eagle Nest.JPG|thumb|right|Nest in a [[Flindersia maculosa|leopardwood]] tree at [[Mutawintji National Park]]]]
Both sexes may participate in building the nest but the female takes the greater share, often standing in the middle and building outwards.<ref name= Brown/> Often wedge-tailed eagles build alternative nest, up to 2 to 3 per territory, though when undisturbed uses the same general site repeatedly.<ref name= Brown/> In Tasmania, territories held a mean of 1.4 nests.<ref name= Olsen/> The nest is usually either substantial or massive. The nest is a structure of sticks ranging {{convert|70|to|100|cm|ft|abbr=on}} across and {{convert|30|to|80|cm|in|abbr=on}} deep when first build but with repeated additions up to {{convert|2.5|m|ft|abbr=on}} across and nearly {{convert|4|m|ft|abbr=on}} deep.<ref name= Ferguson-Lees/><ref name= HBW/> The interior nest cup is commonly around {{convert|30|to|40|cm|in|abbr=on}} across about {{convert|10|cm|in|abbr=on}} deep.<ref name= Brown/> Four studies found the diameter of nests to average from as little as {{convert|1.1|m|ft|abbr=on}} and as much as {{convert|1.9|m|ft|abbr=on}} and in depth from as little as {{convert|1|m|ft|abbr=on}} to as much as {{convert|1.3|m|ft|abbr=on}}. Generally speaking in woodland or forest edge areas, nests tend to be larger, while those in sparser, more arid areas tend to have characteristically smaller nests, as they have lesser access to nest building materials.<ref name= Collins/><ref name= Cherriman/><ref name= Silva/><ref name= Weirsma>{{cite journal|author=Weirsma, J.|author2= Koch, A. J. |year=2012|title=Using surveys of nest characteristics to assess the breeding activity of the Tasmanian wedge-tailed eagle|journal= Corella|volume= 36|issue=2|pages= 38–44}}</ref>

Good sized nests can weigh well over {{convert|400|kg|lb|abbr=on}}.<ref name= Olsen/> Nests are usually lined with green leaves and twigs, a common practice in accipitrids.<ref name= Brown/> Infrequently, they may use an old nest built by another accipitrid, namely [[whistling kite]]s (''Haliastur sphenurus'') and white-bellied sea eagles, with the earlier's nests apparently added to in order to enlargen it.<ref name= Olsen/> Ideally the nest is located at {{convert|12|to|30|m|ft|abbr=on}} above the ground on a lateral branch or main fork of lone or forest tree; in taller trees, nests can be as much as {{convert|75|m|ft|abbr=on}} high to the opposite extreme down in lower ground or even on rocks or ground trees are scarce.
